votes.reason: The Python implementation correctly checks for the existence of three distinct indices whose elements sum to zero, matching the Coq specification's logic.
The Python implementation correctly checks for the existence of three distinct indices whose elements sum to zero, which matches the logic of the Coq specification.
The Python implementation correctly checks for the existence of three distinct indices whose elements sum to zero, which is logically equivalent to the Coq specification.

The Python implementation checks all combinations of three distinct indices (i < j < k) which is sufficient to find any triple with distinct indices summing to zero. The Coq spec requires distinct indices i, j, k (in any order) with sum = 0. Since the Python code checks all ordered combinations, it will find a triple if one exists. The spec function correctly checks all permutations of distinct indices. Both return True iff there exist three distinct indices whose elements sum to zero.

The Python implementation checks for three distinct indices whose elements sum to zero. While triples_sum_to_zero only checks ordered combinations (i < j < k), this is sufficient because if any unordered triple (i, j, k) with distinct indices sums to zero, there exists a corresponding ordered triple that also sums to zero (addition is commutative). The problem_40_spec correctly checks all permutations of distinct indices and verifies the biconditional relationship between the existence of such a triple and output being True.

The Python triples_sum_to_zero function checks for three distinct indices i < j < k where the sum is zero. The Coq spec requires three distinct indices (i != j, i != k, j != k) with sum zero. Since the Python function iterates over all ordered combinations (i < j < k), it covers all cases where three distinct indices exist with sum zero - if any such triple exists with distinct indices in any order, there exists a corresponding ordered triple. The problem_40_spec correctly checks all permutations of distinct indices and verifies the biconditional (exists_triple <-> output=true). Both produce the same concrete results.
Both return True iff there exist three distinct in-range indices whose elements sum to zero. Python’s i<j<k iteration preserves existence, and ints match Coq’s Z. Precondition is True in both.
Both return True iff there exist three distinct in-bounds indices whose elements sum to zero; otherwise False. Integer and index handling match the Coq spec, and the precondition is always True.

Both return True iff there exist three distinct in-bounds indices whose elements sum to zero; otherwise False. The Python loops enforce distinctness and bounds, matching the Coq spec, and the precondition is True.
